zizlfz Notes

    Last updated at March 26, 2026

    Testing Strategy Guide

    Stack: React · Vitest/Jest · React Testing Library · Playwright Audience: Mid-level developers Model: Testing Trophy

    1. Philosophy: The Testing Trophy

    We follow the Testing Trophy model — not the classic pyramid. The key insight is that integration tests give the highest return on investment: they test real user behavior without the cost and fragility of full E2E tests.

            /‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
       /   E2E (few)  \       ← Smoke tests only. 10–20 max.
      /‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
     /Integration (most)\   ← Primary investment. UI + MSW.
    /‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
   /    Unit (as needed)    \  ← Pure functions & complex logic only.
  /‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
 /       Static (always)      \ ← TypeScript, ESLint. Free coverage.
/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\

    Core Principles

    Principle What it means in practice
    E2E as smoke tests Only cover critical happy paths. Not a regression suite.
    Separation of concerns Test backend logic on the backend. Frontend tests own UI behavior only.
    The contract is key Frontend tests verify the UI correctly handles the API's JSON shape and status codes — not the API logic itself.
    Test behavior, not implementation Assert what the user sees, not how the component is structured internally.

    2. Tier Reference

    Tier 1 — E2E Tests (Playwright)

    Purpose: Verify the entire system (Frontend + Backend + DB) works end-to-end for the most critical user journeys.

    When to write one: Ask yourself — "If this breaks silently, would it be a production incident?" If yes, it deserves an E2E test.

    Keep the suite lean: 10–20 tests maximum. Every test you add here is a test that can go flaky and slow down CI.

    Good candidates:

    • User signs up and lands on the dashboard
    • User logs in and sees their data
    • User completes a checkout / form submission flow
    • User resets their password

    Bad candidates (use integration tests instead):

    • Validating a specific error message from the API
    • Testing a disabled button state
    • Testing a loading spinner

    Example — Login happy path:

    // tests/e2e/auth.spec.ts
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('user can log in and reach the dashboard', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/login');

  await page.getByLabel('Email').fill('user@example.com');
  await page.getByLabel('Password').fill('correctpassword');
  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Log in' }).click();

  await expect(page).toHaveURL('/dashboard');
  await expect(page.getByRole('heading', { name: 'Welcome back' })).toBeVisible();
});

    Tier 2 — Integration Tests (Vitest + RTL + MSW) ✅ Primary investment

    Purpose: Verify how the UI handles the full range of API responses — success, errors, loading states, edge cases — without a real network.

    The golden rule: Use Mock Service Worker (MSW) to intercept requests at the network level. This tests the real fetch/axios call in your component, not a mocked function.

    What to test at this tier:

    • Component renders correctly on API success (200)
    • Component shows the right error message on API failure (400, 401, 404, 500)
    • Loading state is shown while a request is in-flight
    • Form validation messages appear correctly
    • User interactions trigger the correct API calls

    MSW setup:

    // src/mocks/handlers.ts
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw';

export const handlers = [
  http.post('/api/login', () => {
    return HttpResponse.json({ token: 'abc123' }, { status: 200 });
  }),
];
    // src/mocks/server.ts
import { setupServer } from 'msw/node';
import { handlers } from './handlers';

export const server = setupServer(...handlers);
    // vitest.setup.ts
import { server } from './src/mocks/server';

beforeAll(() => server.listen());
afterEach(() => server.resetHandlers()); // Reset overrides after each test
afterAll(() => server.close());

    Example — Testing an API error response:

    // src/features/login/LoginForm.test.tsx
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';
import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event';
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw';
import { server } from '../../mocks/server';
import { LoginForm } from './LoginForm';

test('shows a validation error when credentials are invalid', async () => {
  // Override the default handler for this test only
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/login', () => {
      return HttpResponse.json(
        { message: 'Invalid email or password.' },
        { status: 400 }
      );
    })
  );

  render(<LoginForm />);

  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Email'), 'wrong@example.com');
  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Password'), 'wrongpassword');
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Log in' }));

  expect(
    await screen.findByText('Invalid email or password.')
  ).toBeInTheDocument();
});

test('shows a loading state while the request is in-flight', async () => {
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/login', async () => {
      await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 100)); // Simulate delay
      return HttpResponse.json({ token: 'abc123' });
    })
  );

  render(<LoginForm />);

  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Log in' }));

  expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Logging in…' })).toBeDisabled();
});

    Tier 3 — Unit Tests (Vitest)

    Purpose: Test pure functions and complex logic that have no UI or network dependency.

    When to write one: If the logic lives in a standalone function and has branching complexity, edge cases, or transformations that are hard to cover via integration tests — unit test it directly.

    Do not unit test: React components, hooks that make API calls, or anything that requires a rendered DOM. Those belong in Tier 2.

    Good candidates:

    • Password complexity validators
    • Date formatting utilities
    • Price calculation functions
    • Data transformation/mapping functions

    Example:

    // src/utils/passwordValidator.test.ts
import { validatePassword } from './passwordValidator';

describe('validatePassword', () => {
  it('returns invalid if password is shorter than 8 characters', () => {
    expect(validatePassword('short')).toEqual({
      valid: false,
      error: 'Password must be at least 8 characters.',
    });
  });

  it('returns invalid if password has no uppercase letter', () => {
    expect(validatePassword('alllowercase1!')).toEqual({
      valid: false,
      error: 'Password must contain at least one uppercase letter.',
    });
  });

  it('returns valid for a strong password', () => {
    expect(validatePassword('StrongPass1!')).toEqual({ valid: true });
  });
});

    3. The "What Deserves a Test?" Decision Guide

    This is the most common source of disagreement on teams. Use this flowchart as a shared reference.

    Is this pure logic with no UI or network?
  └─ Yes → Unit test it.
  └─ No ↓

Does it involve how the UI responds to an API?
  └─ Yes → Integration test with MSW.
  └─ No ↓

Is it a critical user journey that touches the full stack?
  └─ Yes → E2E test (only if the suite is under 20 tests).
  └─ No → Integration test is probably enough.

    The "Should I test this?" checklist

    Before writing a test, ask:

    • Would a bug here be caught by an existing test? (Don't duplicate coverage.)
    • Am I testing what the user experiences, not how the code is structured?
    • If I refactor this component, will this test still be valid? (It should be.)
    • Am I testing the contract (API shape + status code) rather than the implementation detail?

    4. Flaky E2E Tests — Causes & Fixes

    Flakiness is the #1 reason E2E suites get abandoned. The cause is almost always timing assumptions or shared state.

    Common causes and solutions

    Cause Bad pattern Fix
    Race conditions await page.waitForTimeout(2000) await expect(locator).toBeVisible()
    Hard-coded selectors page.locator('.btn-primary') page.getByRole('button', { name: '...' })
    Test order dependency Tests sharing DB/session state Each test creates its own data
    Brittle network assumptions Real API calls in E2E Use a test environment with seed data

    Playwright best practices

    // ❌ Fragile — assumes 2 seconds is always enough
await page.waitForTimeout(2000);
await expect(page.locator('.success-message')).toBeVisible();

// ✅ Resilient — waits for the condition to be true
await expect(page.getByRole('alert', { name: /success/i })).toBeVisible();
    // ❌ Fragile — breaks on any CSS refactor
page.locator('.form-submit-btn')

// ✅ Resilient — tied to accessible semantics, not styling
page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })

    Isolating test state

    Each E2E test should be independent. Use Playwright's beforeEach to log in, and avoid tests that depend on the outcome of a previous test.

    test.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => {
  // Use a dedicated API route to set up state rather than navigating through UI
  await page.request.post('/api/test-setup/seed-user', {
    data: { email: 'test@example.com', password: 'TestPass1!' }
  });
});

    5. CI Pipeline Strategy

    Slow CI discourages thorough testing. Structure your pipeline to give fast feedback first.

    Recommended pipeline order

    [1] Static analysis      ~10s   TypeScript, ESLint            ← Fastest feedback
[2] Unit tests           ~30s   Vitest                        ← Fail fast on logic errors
[3] Integration tests    ~2min  Vitest + RTL + MSW            ← Primary regression gate
[4] E2E tests            ~5min  Playwright (headless)         ← Run last; most expensive

    Keeping E2E fast

    • Run E2E tests in parallel. Playwright supports this natively via workers config.
    • Only run E2E on main / PR merges, not on every commit to feature branches.
    • Cap the suite at 20 tests. If you're exceeding this, convert candidates to integration tests.
    // playwright.config.ts
export default defineConfig({
  fullyParallel: true,
  workers: process.env.CI ? 4 : undefined,
  retries: process.env.CI ? 1 : 0, // One retry in CI only; never locally
  use: {
    baseURL: process.env.BASE_URL ?? 'http://localhost:3000',
  },
});

    Vitest configuration for speed

    // vitest.config.ts
export default defineConfig({
  test: {
    environment: 'jsdom',
    setupFiles: ['./vitest.setup.ts'],
    pool: 'threads',       // Faster than forks for most test suites
    coverage: {
      reporter: ['text', 'lcov'],
      exclude: ['**/*.test.tsx', '**/mocks/**'],
    },
  },
});

    6. Coverage Guidelines

    Coverage numbers are a signal, not a goal. Don't chase 100%.

    Area Target Rationale
    Utility functions 90–100% Pure logic; easy and high-value to cover fully
    UI components 70–80% Focus on user-facing behavior, not every internal branch
    E2E critical paths 100% of defined happy paths If it's in scope, it must have a test
    Third-party integrations Minimal Test the contract, not the library

    A component with 60% coverage that tests the right behaviors is more valuable than one with 95% coverage that only tests implementation details.

    7. Quick Reference

    Which tool for which job

    Scenario Tool
    Validate a utility function Vitest unit test
    Test a form shows the right error from a 400 response Vitest + RTL + MSW
    Test a loading spinner appears Vitest + RTL + MSW
    Test a user can complete checkout end-to-end Playwright
    Test TypeScript types are correct tsc --noEmit in CI

    Query priority in React Testing Library

    Always prefer queries that reflect what a user actually perceives:

    1. getByRole          ← Preferred. Accessible and resilient.
2. getByLabelText     ← Great for form fields.
3. getByPlaceholderText
4. getByText
5. getByDisplayValue
6. getByAltText
7. getByTitle
8. getByTestId        ← Last resort. Avoid unless nothing else works.

    9. Feature Scenario Map: Checkout Flow

    This section shows how to systematically break a feature into all its scenarios and assign the right test tier to each one. Use this as a template when planning tests for any new feature.

    Scenario inventory

    # Scenario Trigger Expected outcome Test tier
    1 Successful checkout Valid card, items in cart, user logged in Order confirmed, redirect to /order-confirmation E2E
    2 Payment declined Card processor returns decline Error banner shown, user stays on checkout Integration
    3 Network failure API returns 500 Generic error shown, retry button visible Integration
    4 Session expired mid-checkout API returns 401 Redirect to /login?redirect=/checkout Integration
    5 Cart is empty User navigates to /checkout directly Redirect to /cart Integration
    6 Invalid promo code POST /apply-promo returns 400 Inline error shown under promo field Integration
    7 Valid promo code POST /apply-promo returns 200 Discount applied, total updates Integration
    8 Loading state API call in-flight Submit button disabled and shows spinner Integration
    9 Price calculation logic Free function calculateOrderTotal() Subtotal + tax + shipping = total Unit
    10 Promo code format validation Free function isValidPromoFormat() Rejects blank/short codes before API call Unit

    Scenario diagram

    Legend: 🟢 Success outcome (E2E or integration success path) · 🟡 Redirect outcome · 🔴 Error outcome shown to user

    Test implementations

    Scenario 1 — Happy path (E2E · Playwright)

    // tests/e2e/checkout.spec.ts
test('user completes checkout successfully', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/checkout');

  await page.getByLabel('Card number').fill('4242424242424242');
  await page.getByLabel('Expiry').fill('12/28');
  await page.getByLabel('CVC').fill('123');
  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Place order' }).click();

  await expect(page).toHaveURL('/order-confirmation');
  await expect(page.getByRole('heading', { name: 'Order confirmed' })).toBeVisible();
});

    Scenario 2 — Payment declined (Integration · Vitest + RTL + MSW)

    // src/features/checkout/Checkout.test.tsx
test('shows a declined error when the card is rejected', async () => {
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/orders', () =>
      HttpResponse.json({ message: 'Your card was declined.' }, { status: 402 })
    )
  );

  render(<Checkout />, { wrapper: Providers });
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Place order' }));

  expect(
    await screen.findByRole('alert', { name: /card was declined/i })
  ).toBeInTheDocument();

  // User stays on checkout — no redirect
  expect(mockNavigate).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
});

    Scenario 4 — Session expired, redirect to login (Integration)

    test('redirects to login with return path when session has expired', async () => {
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/orders', () =>
      HttpResponse.json({ message: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 })
    )
  );

  render(<Checkout />, { wrapper: Providers });
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Place order' }));

  await waitFor(() => {
    expect(mockNavigate).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/login?redirect=/checkout');
  });
});

    Scenario 5 — Empty cart redirects (Integration)

    test('redirects to cart page when cart is empty', async () => {
  // Render with empty cart state
  render(<Checkout />, {
    wrapper: ({ children }) => (
      <Providers initialCart={[]}>
        {children}
      </Providers>
    ),
  });

  await waitFor(() => {
    expect(mockNavigate).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/cart');
  });
});

    Scenario 8 — Loading state (Integration)

    test('disables submit button and shows spinner while order is processing', async () => {
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/orders', async () => {
      await delay(200); // Simulate network latency
      return HttpResponse.json({ orderId: 'abc123' });
    })
  );

  render(<Checkout />, { wrapper: Providers });

  const button = screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Place order' });
  await userEvent.click(button);

  // Mid-request: button should be disabled with loading text
  expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /processing/i })).toBeDisabled();

  // After response: button returns to normal
  await waitFor(() => {
    expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Place order' })).not.toBeDisabled();
  });
});

    Scenario 6 — Invalid promo code (Integration)

    test('shows inline error under promo field when code is invalid', async () => {
  server.use(
    http.post('/api/apply-promo', () =>
      HttpResponse.json({ message: 'Promo code not found.' }, { status: 400 })
    )
  );

  render(<Checkout />, { wrapper: Providers });

  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Promo code'), 'BADCODE');
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Apply' }));

  expect(
    await screen.findByText('Promo code not found.')
  ).toBeInTheDocument();
});

    Scenarios 9 & 10 — Pure logic (Unit · Vitest)

    // src/utils/orderCalculations.test.ts
describe('calculateOrderTotal', () => {
  it('sums subtotal, tax, and shipping correctly', () => {
    expect(calculateOrderTotal({
      subtotal: 100,
      taxRate: 0.1,
      shippingCost: 5,
    })).toBe(115);
  });

  it('applies a percentage discount before calculating tax', () => {
    expect(calculateOrderTotal({
      subtotal: 100,
      discountPercent: 20,
      taxRate: 0.1,
      shippingCost: 5,
    })).toBe(93); // (100 - 20) * 1.1 + 5
  });
});

describe('isValidPromoFormat', () => {
  it('rejects codes shorter than 4 characters', () => {
    expect(isValidPromoFormat('AB')).toBe(false);
  });

  it('rejects empty strings', () => {
    expect(isValidPromoFormat('')).toBe(false);
  });

  it('accepts a valid alphanumeric code', () => {
    expect(isValidPromoFormat('SAVE20')).toBe(true);
  });
});

    The planning habit

    Before writing a single line of test code for any feature, fill in this table:

    | Scenario | What triggers it | Expected outcome | Tier |

    If you find yourself writing more than 2–3 E2E tests for a single feature, that's a signal most of them should be integration tests instead.

    8. Keeping MSW Mocks in Sync with the Backend API

    This is one of the most common failure modes for integration tests: the mock drifts from reality, tests stay green, but production breaks.

    The core problem

    Stack How drift is prevented
    Full TypeScript monorepo Shared type definitions. Frontend won't compile if the API contract changes.
    Go backend + React TS No shared types. Drift is invisible until runtime. This is our situation.

    The solution is to treat the API schema as the single source of truth and generate TypeScript types from it automatically. When the Go backend changes the schema, the frontend types regenerate — and if the frontend code or MSW handlers don't match, TypeScript will refuse to compile.

    Recommended approach: OpenAPI as the contract

    Go backend  ──generates──▶  openapi.yaml  ──generates──▶  TypeScript types
                                │                                │
                                │                                ▼
                                │                        MSW handlers typed
                                │                        against these types
                                ▼
                        Single source of truth
                        committed to the repo

    Step 1 — Generate an OpenAPI spec from the Go backend

    Use swaggo/swag to annotate your Go handlers and generate openapi.yaml:

    // In your Go handler:
// @Summary      Log in a user
// @Accept       json
// @Produce      json
// @Param        body  body      LoginRequest  true  "Credentials"
// @Success      200   {object}  LoginResponse
// @Failure      400   {object}  ErrorResponse
// @Router       /api/login [post]
func (h *Handler) Login(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { ... }
    # Run in the Go repo to regenerate the spec
swag init --output ./docs

    The output (docs/swagger.yaml) is committed to the repo and becomes the contract both sides agree on.

    Step 2 — Generate TypeScript types from the spec

    In the frontend repo, use openapi-typescript to generate types:

    npm install -D openapi-typescript
npx openapi-typescript ../backend/docs/swagger.yaml -o src/types/api.generated.ts

    Add this as an npm script so it's easy to re-run:

    // package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "generate:api-types": "openapi-typescript ../backend/docs/swagger.yaml -o src/types/api.generated.ts"
  }
}

    The generated file looks like this (you never edit it manually):

    // src/types/api.generated.ts  ← auto-generated, do not edit
export interface paths {
  "/api/login": {
    post: {
      requestBody: {
        content: {
          "application/json": {
            email: string;
            password: string;
          };
        };
      };
      responses: {
        200: { content: { "application/json": { token: string; userId: string } } };
        400: { content: { "application/json": { message: string } } };
      };
    };
  };
}

    Step 3 — Type your MSW handlers against generated types

    Use openapi-fetch as your fetch client, which binds directly to the generated types. Then type your MSW handlers against the same source:

    // src/mocks/handlers.ts
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw';
import type { paths } from '../types/api.generated';

// Extract the exact response shape from the generated types
type LoginSuccess = paths['/api/login']['post']['responses'][200]['content']['application/json'];
type LoginError   = paths['/api/login']['post']['responses'][400]['content']['application/json'];

export const handlers = [
  http.post('/api/login', (): HttpResponse => {
    const body: LoginSuccess = { token: 'mock-token', userId: '123' };
    return HttpResponse.json(body, { status: 200 });
  }),
];

    Now if the Go team renames token to accessToken in the spec, you regenerate the types, and the MSW handler above fails to compile — forcing a fix before the code ever ships.

    Enforcing this in CI

    The key risk is that someone changes the Go backend, regenerates the spec, but forgets to regenerate the frontend types. Catch this automatically:

    # .github/workflows/check-api-types.yml
- name: Regenerate API types
  run: npm run generate:api-types

- name: Fail if generated types are out of date
  run: |
    git diff --exit-code src/types/api.generated.ts \
    || (echo "API types are out of date. Run 'npm run generate:api-types' and commit." && exit 1)

    This CI step regenerates the types and checks whether the committed file matches. If the backend spec changed but the frontend types weren't updated, the build fails with a clear message.

    Lightweight alternative: Zod schemas as the contract

    If setting up OpenAPI feels like too much overhead for your current stage, a practical middle ground is to define Zod schemas for each API response in the frontend and use them to validate both your MSW mock responses and runtime API responses.

    // src/schemas/auth.ts
import { z } from 'zod';

export const LoginResponseSchema = z.object({
  token: z.string(),
  userId: z.string(),
});

export const LoginErrorSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string(),
});

export type LoginResponse = z.infer<typeof LoginResponseSchema>;

    Use the schema in your API layer to validate real responses at runtime:

    // src/api/auth.ts
const raw = await fetch('/api/login', { ... }).then(r => r.json());
const parsed = LoginResponseSchema.safeParse(raw);

if (!parsed.success) {
  // The backend changed shape — this surfaces immediately in dev/staging
  console.error('API contract mismatch:', parsed.error);
}

    Use the same schema in your MSW handler to keep the mock honest:

    // src/mocks/handlers.ts
import { LoginResponseSchema } from '../schemas/auth';

http.post('/api/login', () => {
  const body: z.infer<typeof LoginResponseSchema> = {
    token: 'mock-token',
    userId: '123',
  };
  return HttpResponse.json(body);
});

    The tradeoff: Zod schemas are still hand-written and can drift. They rely on developers updating them when the backend changes, whereas the OpenAPI approach makes that a CI failure. Use Zod as a stepping stone; migrate to OpenAPI codegen as the team and API stabilise.

    Which approach to use?

    OpenAPI codegen Zod schemas
    Drift prevention Automatic — CI fails if out of sync Manual — relies on developer discipline
    Setup cost Medium (needs swaggo + openapi-typescript) Low (just install zod)
    Runtime validation Not built-in (add separately) Yes, built-in via safeParse
    Best for Stable, documented APIs with multiple consumers Early-stage APIs or small teams

    Start with Zod if the API is still in flux. Graduate to OpenAPI codegen once endpoints stabilise.

    MSW handler override pattern

    // In your test file — override a handler just for one test
server.use(
  http.get('/api/user', () => {
    return HttpResponse.json({ error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 });
  })
);
// server.resetHandlers() in afterEach restores the default automatically
    © 2026. All rights reserved.
